nodes.x: AND now it came to pass in the commencement of the twenty and ninth year of the judges, that Ammoron sent unto Moroni desiring that he would exchange prisoners.

1-3 Ammoron sent to Moroni desiring that he would exchange prisoners. In the Twenty-ninth year of the Judges, King Ammoron sent a request to Moroni that both sides of the impending conflict exchange the prisoners that each had taken. Ammoron's suggestion which was conveyed by a Lamanite messenger caused Moroni to rejoice, for in it he saw the amelioration of a burden that had become extremely…
And it came to pass that Moroni felt to rejoice exceedingly at this request, for he desired the provisions which were imparted for the support of the Lamanite prisoners for the support of his own…
Now the Lamanites had taken many women and children, and there was not a woman nor a child among all the prisoners of Moroni, or the prisoners whom Moroni had taken; therefore Moroni resolved upon a…
Therefore he wrote an epistle, and sent it by the servant of Ammoron, the same who had brought an epistle to Moroni. Now these are the words which he wrote unto Ammoron, saying:

4-14 Moroni wrote an epistle to Ammoron. While the messenger from Ammoron waited for a reply to his master's request, Moroni, undoubtedly with his advisor's counsel, wrote and later dispatched by this same courier his answer to Ammoron. Moroni's answer was a call for Ammoron to repent, and cease his
Behold, Ammoron, I have written unto you somewhat concerning this war which ye have waged against my people, or rather which thy brother hath waged against them, and which ye are still determined to…
Behold, I would tell you somewhat concerning the justice of God, and the sword of his almighty wrath, which doth hang over you except ye repent and withdraw your armies into your own lands, or the…
Yea, I would tell you these things if ye were capable of hearkening unto them; yea, I would tell you concerning that awful hell that awaits to receive such murderers as thou and thy brother have…
But as ye have once rejected these things, and have fought against the people of the Lord, even so I may expect you will do it again.
And now behold, we are prepared to receive you; yea, and except you withdraw your purposes, behold, ye will pull down the wrath of that God whom you have rejected upon you, even to your utter…

Fear God More Than Armies Ammoron and Moroni negotiate for the exchange of prisoners-Moroni demands that the Lamanites withdraw and cease their murderous attacks-Ammoron demands that the Nephites lay down their arms and become subject to the Lamanites (Alma 54 headnote). Warning: It is a federal offense to read other people's mail. Nevertheless, we get to read a couple of other people's letters…
But, as the Lord liveth, our armies shall come upon you except ye withdraw, and ye shall soon be visited with death, for we will retain our cities and our lands; yea, and we will maintain our…
But behold, it supposeth me that I talk to you concerning these things in vain; or it supposeth me that thou art a child of hell; therefore I will close my epistle by telling you that I will not…

And behold, if ye do not this, I will come against you with my armies; yea, even I will arm my women and my children, and I will come against you, and I will follow you even into your own land, which…

Behold, I am in my anger, and also my people; ye have sought to murder us, and we have only sought to defend ourselves. But behold, if ye seek to destroy us more we will seek to destroy you; yea, and…

13-14 Ye have sought to murder us, and we have only sought to defend ourselves. In spite of the fact, we conceive of Moroni saying, that we have only sought to defend ourselves against your wanton desire to slay us, and notwithstanding that so far the battles of this war have been fought solely around our own homes and firesides, you, Moroni again warned Ammoron, need not think that we are weak,…
Now I close my epistle. I am Moroni; I am a leader of the people of the Nephites.
Now it came to pass that Ammoron, when he had received this epistle, was angry; and he wrote another epistle unto Moroni, and these are the words which he wrote, saying:
I am Ammoron, the king of the Lamanites; I am the brother of Amalickiah whom ye have murdered. Behold, I will avenge his blood upon you, yea, and I will come upon you with my armies for I fear not…

15-24 I am Ammoron, the King of the Lamanites. Ammoron's character is clearly set forth in the angry reply that he sent to Moroni upon receiving the one from him. He ignored facts that even to him were known, and substituted falsehoods therefor. He resorted to the base calumnies of other apostates who beforetime had used them to stir up the hatreds and angry passions of past generations of…
For behold, your fathers did wrong their brethren, insomuch that they did rob them of their right to the government when it rightly belonged unto them.

Your fathers did wrong their brethren. Ammoron repeated the old canard that had been held true in the traditions of the Lamanites, that the government of the people, upon the death of Lehi, the noble sire of both races, had been wrested by Nephi from Laman and Lemuel whose right it was to rule. He imagined that to argue thus he made a reasonable case against the descendants of those who, he said,…

And now behold, if ye will lay down your arms, and subject yourselves to be governed by those to whom the government doth rightly belong, then will I cause that my people shall lay down their weapons…

18-20 If ye will . . . subject yourselves to be governed by those to whom the government doth rightly belong. . . . Again Ammoron showed his perfidy by suggesting to Moroni that the Nephites lay down their arms, and subject themselves to be ruled by their foes. This amounted to unconditional surrender by Moroni, and would have meant the abrogation by the Nephites of all the rights and privileges…
Behold, ye have breathed out many threatenings against me and my people; but behold, we fear not your threatenings.
Nevertheless, I will grant to exchange prisoners according to your request, gladly, that I may preserve my food for my men of war; and we will wage a war which shall be eternal, either to the…
And as concerning that God whom ye say we have rejected, behold, we know not such a being; neither do ye; but if it so be that there is such a being, we know not but that he hath made us as well as…
And if it so be that there is a devil and a hell, behold will he not send you there to dwell with my brother whom ye have murdered, whom ye have hinted that he hath gone to such a place? But behold…

21-22 Concerning that God whom ye say we have rejected. In words that portended failure for his cause, Ammoron defied God, and set at naught His power to destroy him. Not only did he deny His existence, but also did he decry any knowledge Moroni had of Him. Ammoron, in a stupor caused by ignorance, self-righteousness, and infatuation, failed to see the power of the Nephites that by God was…
I am Ammoron, and a descendant of Zoram, whom your fathers pressed and brought out of Jerusalem.
And behold now, I am a bold Lamanite; behold, this war hath been waged to avenge their wrongs, and to maintain and to obtain their rights to the government; and I close my epistle to Moroni.
